question: Why do we test software? What is the goal of testing?
answer:
Testing finds defects before users do, reduces risk, and builds confidence that the product meets requirements. The goal is not to prove the software is perfect — it is to provide information about quality so the team can decide whether to ship.

  • Catch bugs early (cheaper to fix).
  • Verify behaviour against requirements.
  • Prevent regressions when code changes.
  • Improve trust for release decisions.
tip: Say: testing reduces risk and informs release decisions — it does not guarantee zero bugs.
deep:
Beginner framing

Think of testing like checking a car before a long trip: you are not trying to invent a perfect car in the garage — you are finding what is broken or risky before customers drive it.

What testing is (and is not)

  • Is: a process of evaluating software against expected behaviour and risk.
  • Is not: a promise that every bug is found. Exhaustive testing of real systems is impossible.

Why interviews love this

Junior answers stop at "to find bugs". Stronger answers add: cost of late defects, regression safety, and helping product/business decide release readiness.

How to say it in an interview

“We test to find defects early, confirm the product matches requirements, and give the team evidence to ship with acceptable risk. Testing never proves zero bugs — it reduces uncertainty.”

question: Verification vs Validation?
answer:
Verification asks: Are we building the product right? — checks against specs/design (reviews, static analysis, unit tests against requirements).

Validation asks: Are we building the right product? — checks whether it satisfies the real user need (UAT, usability, acceptance testing).
tip: Memory aid: Verification = specs; Validation = user/business need.
deep:
Simple example

  • Verification: requirement says checkout must apply a 10% coupon — your test confirms the discount math and API response.
  • Validation: users in UAT say the coupon field is confusing and they abandon checkout — product is "correct" but not the right experience.

Where each happens

VerificationValidation
QuestionBuilt correctly?Useful / acceptable?
AgainstRequirements, design, code standardsUser needs, business goals
ExamplesCode review, unit/API tests, walkthroughsUAT, beta, usability testing

How to say it in an interview

“Verification checks we implemented the spec correctly; validation checks we solved the real user problem. A feature can pass verification and still fail validation.”

question: Functional vs non-functional testing?
answer:
Functional checks what the system does — features, business rules, inputs/outputs (login works, order is created, invoice total is correct).

Non-functional checks how the system behaves — performance, security, usability, reliability, compatibility.
tip: Functional = correct behaviour; non-functional = quality attributes under real conditions.
deep:
Examples you can name

  • Functional: place order, apply coupon, cancel subscription, role-based access to a page.
  • Non-functional: page loads under 2s for 1k users, password storage is hashed, app works on Chrome/Firefox, system recovers after crash.

Why SDETs care

Many teams automate functional API/UI flows first. Non-functional work (load, security) often needs specialised tools and environments — mention that you know the difference even if your current role is mostly functional automation.

How to say it in an interview

“Functional testing verifies features against business rules. Non-functional testing verifies quality attributes like speed, security, and usability. Both are needed for release confidence.”

question: Explain the main test levels: unit, integration, system, acceptance.
answer:
• Unit — smallest piece of code (method/class) in isolation; fast; usually by developers.
  • Integration — modules/services working together (API + DB, service A calling service B).
  • System — the full application as a whole against requirements.
  • Acceptance (UAT) — business/users confirm it is ready for real use.

You need all levels; relying only on UI/system tests is slow and brittle.
tip: Map each bug to the lowest level that could have caught it — that is seniority.
deep:
Who usually owns what

LevelTypical ownerExample
UnitDeveloperDiscount calculator returns 10%
IntegrationDev / SDETOrder API writes a row in DB
System / E2ESDET / QAUser completes checkout in UI
AcceptanceBusiness / UAT usersClient signs off release

Common anti-pattern

Only E2E UI tests: suite is slow, flaky, and hard to debug. Better: many fast lower-level checks + fewer critical E2E paths.

How to say it in an interview

“Unit catches logic bugs cheaply, integration catches wiring bugs, system confirms the whole product, and acceptance confirms business readiness. As an SDET I automate heavily at API/UI system level, but I still push for unit coverage on complex rules.”

question: What is shift-left testing?
answer:
Shift-left means starting quality activities earlier in the lifecycle — during requirements, design, and development — instead of only testing at the end.

Examples: review requirements for testability, write acceptance criteria with the story, automate unit/API tests in the PR pipeline, pair with developers on edge cases.
tip: Finding a requirement bug in planning is cheaper than finding it in UAT.
deep:
Why it matters

Defect cost grows over time. A missing requirement found in production can mean hotfixes, data repair, and lost trust. Shift-left moves feedback closer to the change.

Concrete SDET behaviours

  • Ask clarifying questions in grooming ("What if payment fails mid-order?").
  • Add API tests for new endpoints in the same sprint.
  • Block merges when critical automated checks fail (CI).
  • Share bug patterns so developers prevent repeats.

How to say it in an interview

“Shift-left means I don’t wait for a ‘QA phase’. I get involved in requirements, automate early, and put fast checks in CI so defects are found when they are cheapest to fix.”

question: Positive testing vs negative testing?
answer:
Positive testing uses valid inputs and happy-path flows to confirm the system works as expected (correct login, successful order).

Negative testing uses invalid/unexpected inputs to confirm the system handles failure safely (wrong password, missing required field, SQL injection attempt).
tip: Interviewers expect both: prove it works, and prove it fails gracefully.
deep:
Examples

  • Positive: valid user + correct password → dashboard.
  • Negative: valid user + wrong password → clear error, no session created.
  • Negative: quantity = -1 or "abc" → validation error, no order placed.

Why negative tests catch expensive bugs

Many production incidents are edge cases: empty payloads, timeouts, duplicate submit clicks, expired tokens. Negative testing is how you design those scenarios on purpose.

How to say it in an interview

“Positive tests confirm the happy path. Negative tests confirm invalid input is rejected safely — with the right message, status code, and no bad side effects like partial orders.”

question: What is STLC (Software Testing Life Cycle)?
answer: STLC is the structured process QA follows from planning to closure. Common phases: Requirement Analysis → Test Planning → Test Case Design → Environment Setup → Test Execution → Defect Reporting/Retest → Test Closure.
tip: STLC runs alongside SDLC — testing is not only an end phase.
deep:
Phases with a simple example (ecommerce login)

  1. Requirement analysis — login must support email/password and lock after 5 failures.
  2. Planning — scope, tools (Postman/Selenium), entry/exit criteria.
  3. Design — write positive/negative cases, prepare data.
  4. Environment — QA URL, test users, browser matrix.
  5. Execution — run manual + automated packs.
  6. Closure — summary report, open risks, lessons learned.

How to say it in an interview

“STLC is how testing is organised end-to-end. I start from requirements, plan risk-based coverage, design cases, execute, track defects, and close with a release quality summary.”

question: How does testing differ in Waterfall vs Agile?
answer: In Waterfall, testing is mostly a late phase after development is "done". In Agile, testing happens continuously every sprint — stories are tested as they are built, with automation and frequent regression.
tip: For 4–5 YOE roles, emphasise Agile collaboration, not only end-phase QA.
deep:
Practical difference

  • Waterfall: long test cycle, heavy documentation, late feedback.
  • Agile: Definition of Done includes testing, daily collaboration with devs, automate early, demo every sprint.

Example

In Agile, a "Place Order" story is tested in the same sprint with API checks + critical UI path, not waiting for a final QA month.

How to say it in an interview

“In Agile I’m embedded with the team each sprint — clarifying acceptance criteria, testing increments, and automating critical paths so regression stays cheap.”

question: Retesting vs Regression testing?
answer: Retesting checks whether a specific defect fix works (run the failed case again). Regression checks that the fix (or any change) did not break existing features.
tip: Retest the bug; regress the neighbourhood and critical paths.
deep:
Example

Bug: coupon 10% not applied. After fix:

  • Retest: apply coupon CODE10 → total reduced correctly.
  • Regression: checkout without coupon, expired coupon, stack with free-shipping still behaves correctly.

How to say it in an interview

“Retesting confirms the fix. Regression confirms we didn’t break nearby behaviour. I always do both before closing a defect.”

question: Static testing vs Dynamic testing?
answer: Static testing finds issues without executing code — reviews of requirements, design, test cases, code walkthroughs. Dynamic testing executes the software — manual/automated functional tests, API calls, UI flows.
tip: Reviewing a vague requirement is static testing and often prevents expensive bugs.
deep:
Examples

  • Static: requirement says "user can upload files" but no size/type limit — raise ambiguity before coding.
  • Dynamic: upload 25MB exe and verify rejection.

How to say it in an interview

“Static testing catches defects in documents and design early. Dynamic testing validates running behaviour. I do both — especially requirement reviews before automation.”

question: Black-box vs White-box vs Grey-box testing?
answer:
• Black-box — test using requirements/UI/API behaviour only; no code knowledge.
  • White-box — test with code knowledge (branches, paths, unit tests).
  • Grey-box — mix: know architecture/DB/API contracts while testing like a user (common for SDETs).
tip: Most SDET work is grey-box: UI/API tests guided by system knowledge.
deep:
Ecommerce example

  • Black-box: place order in UI, check confirmation page.
  • White-box: unit-test discount calculation branches.
  • Grey-box: place order via API, then SQL-check order + payment rows.

How to say it in an interview

“I often work grey-box — I design scenarios from requirements but validate with API responses and DB state, not only what the UI shows.”

question: What are entry and exit criteria in testing?
answer: Entry criteria = conditions before testing starts (build deployed, smoke passed, test data ready). Exit criteria = conditions to stop/finish (planned cases executed, critical bugs closed, coverage/risk accepted).
tip: Exit criteria protect you from "we tested forever" and from shipping with open blockers.
deep:
Example entry

  • QA environment stable.
  • Build version known.
  • Critical configs/feature flags set.

Example exit

  • 100% smoke + 95% high-priority cases executed.
  • No open Sev-1/Sev-2 defects (or formally waived).
  • Regression pack green.

How to say it in an interview

“Entry criteria tell me the build is testable. Exit criteria tell stakeholders when quality is good enough to release, based on risk — not just a calendar date.”

question: Test Plan vs Test Strategy?
answer: Test Strategy is the high-level approach (what types of testing, tools, environments, risk focus) — often organisation/project wide. Test Plan is a concrete document for a release/sprint: scope, schedule, resources, deliverables, entry/exit criteria.
tip: Strategy = approach; Plan = schedule and specifics for this release.
deep:
Tiny examples

  • Strategy: "API-first automation, UI for critical journeys, SQL validation for billing."
  • Plan: "Release 24.6 — test Order Management module, 3 SDETs, Jenkins smoke on every build, UAT window Fri–Mon."

How to say it in an interview

“Strategy defines how we assure quality. The plan applies that strategy to a specific release with scope, owners, and exit criteria.”

question: What is risk-based testing?
answer: Risk-based testing prioritises test effort where failure would hurt most — high business impact × high likelihood. You test payment/order paths deeper than a rarely used settings tooltip.
tip: With limited time, risk-based testing is how seniors decide what to test first.
deep:
Simple scoring example

AreaImpactLikelihoodPriority
Checkout paymentHighMediumTest deeply + automate
Order cancelHighMediumHigh
Profile avatar cropLowLowLight/manual

How to say it in an interview

“I prioritise by business risk. For ecommerce order management I protect money and fulfillment paths first, then secondary UX polish if time remains.”

question: What is compatibility testing?
answer: Compatibility testing checks the product works across supported combinations — browsers, OS versions, devices, or dependent systems (browser × OS, mobile screen sizes, integrations).
tip: Always test against the agreed support matrix, not every browser ever made.
deep:
Example matrix

  • Chrome + Windows (primary)
  • Edge + Windows
  • Safari + macOS (if required)

You might automate critical paths on primary browser and spot-check secondary ones.

How to say it in an interview

“I validate against the supported matrix. Critical journeys run on primary browsers in CI; secondary browsers get smoke/sanity based on risk.”

question: What is exploratory testing? When do you use it?
answer: Exploratory testing is simultaneous learning, test design, and execution — you investigate the product with a charter instead of only following scripted cases. Use it for new features, weak requirements, or after major UI changes to find unexpected bugs.
tip: Exploratory complements scripted tests — it does not replace regression packs.
deep:
Example charter

"Explore coupon stacking on checkout for 45 minutes; focus on combining percentage + free shipping; note any total mismatches."

How to say it in an interview

“I use exploratory testing to discover edge cases scripts miss, then convert valuable findings into reusable regression cases or automation.”

question: Which quality/testing metrics are useful? Which are misleading?
answer: Useful: escaped defects, defect leakage by severity, automation pass rate trends, requirement coverage of critical paths, mean time to detect/fix. Misleading as sole goals: raw case count executed, vanity 100% coverage, "number of bugs found" without context.
tip: Metrics should drive decisions, not gaming.
deep:
Good metric example

"Sev-1 escaped to prod this quarter: 1 → investigate root cause (missing API negative test)."

Bad metric example

"We executed 2000 cases" — says nothing about risk covered.

How to say it in an interview

“I track defect leakage and stability of critical automated packs. I avoid optimising for case count or fake coverage percentages.”

question: What makes a good test environment?
answer: A good test environment is stable, versioned, and close enough to production for the risks you care about: correct configs, integrated dependencies (or reliable mocks), known test data, and access to logs/DB for debugging.
tip: Many "product bugs" are environment/config issues — verify build + config first.
deep:
Checklist

  • Known build number.
  • Test users/roles ready.
  • Payment/email stubs configured if needed.
  • DB access for validation.
  • Monitoring/logs available.

How to say it in an interview

“Before deep testing I confirm environment readiness — build, config, data, and integrations. Otherwise failures are noisy and hard to trust.”

question: What is a Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)?
answer: An RTM maps requirements/user stories to test cases (and sometimes defects). It shows coverage gaps — every critical requirement should have at least one test, and every test should trace to a need.
tip: RTM answers: "Are we testing what matters?"
code:
Req-Login-01 → TC-01, TC-02, TC-03
Req-Order-05 → TC-40, API-12 (missing UI e2e? → gap)
deep:
Example

Story: "User can cancel order within 1 hour." RTM links to cases: cancel via UI, cancel via API, cancel after 61 minutes rejected, payment refund status validated in DB.

How to say it in an interview

“I use traceability to ensure critical requirements aren’t missed and to show stakeholders exactly what evidence covers each story.”

question: Quality Assurance (QA) vs Quality Control (QC)?
answer: QA is process-oriented — preventing defects (standards, reviews, shift-left, good Definition of Done). QC is product-oriented — detecting defects in the build (test execution, inspections of outputs).
tip: Testing is largely QC; improving how the team builds quality is QA.
deep:
Examples

  • QA: add acceptance criteria template; require API tests in PR.
  • QC: execute regression and log defects.

How to say it in an interview

“QC finds defects in the product. QA improves the process so fewer defects are created. As an SDET I do both — execute tests and strengthen automation/CI quality gates.”
